skill.name: azure-keyvault-keys-ts
skill.description: Build TypeScript workflows for Azure Key Vault key management, cryptographic operations, rotation policies, and related secret examples using Azure SDK packages.
skill.summary: Use Azure SDK examples to manage Key Vault keys and crypto operations in TypeScript.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external-command and critical combination findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and documentation examples, not executable skill code. The skill is still medium risk because it teaches live Azure Key Vault operations, including printing a secret value and permanently purging keys or secrets. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Secret Value Printed To Console
medium_findings.description: The documentation example retrieves a Key Vault secret and prints secret.value to stdout. This is not malicious code, but users who copy it could expose credentials in logs, terminals, or CI output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact example logs secret.value after reading a secret. Confidence is high because the behavior is clear, although it is documentation rather than automatically executed code.

medium_findings.title: Permanent Key And Secret Purge Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples include purgeDeletedSecret and purgeDeletedKey calls after soft delete. These are legitimate Azure operations, but copied use can cause irreversible loss of protected material.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The purge calls are directly shown in the skill text. Confidence is high because the destructive behavior is explicit and matches Azure Key Vault semantics.

medium_findings.title: Secret Backup And Restore Handling
medium_findings.description: The backup and restore section handles secret backups in addition to key backups. This is legitimate administration guidance, but it increases sensitivity because backup blobs can move protected material between contexts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly backs up and restores a secret. Confidence is high for sensitive handling risk, but lower than destructive purge because the operation can be legitimate in controlled recovery workflows.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections map to Markdown code fences for bash and TypeScript examples. The skill file does not contain executable Ruby backticks or a script that runs arbitrary shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows the flagged patterns are fenced documentation blocks. Confidence is very high because there is no runnable wrapper around these examples.

low_findings.title: Azure Vault URL And Environment Variable Use Are Legitimate
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL pattern is an Azure Key Vault endpoint template, and process.env is used to construct the vault URL. This is expected SDK configuration, not evidence of credential harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL points to the standard vault.azure.net service and uses a placeholder vault name. Confidence is high because the context is normal Azure SDK setup.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File And Weak Crypto Static Findings Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The environment-file finding refers to process.env usage, not reading a .env file. The certificate/key, weak algorithm, and dangerous-combination alerts are not confirmed; reviewed examples show documentation for Azure SDK setup and common algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show metadata, environment variable access, key metadata output, and common Azure-supported algorithms. Confidence is high because the dangerous combination is based on false-positive inputs, though algorithm strength still depends on user policy choices.

dangerous_patterns.title: Logging Retrieved Secret Material
dangerous_patterns.description: A copied example can disclose secret values through console output. Users should redact values or verify only secret metadata.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path clearly reads and logs the secret value. Confidence is high because the sensitive sink is explicit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Irreversible Purge Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes permanent purge calls for deleted secrets and keys. These operations should require explicit safeguards and production change controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The purge functions are direct Azure SDK calls. Confidence is high because the lines show the risky operation names and surrounding delete flow.

content.user_title: Manage Azure Key Vault Keys in TypeScript
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable examples for key creation, rotation, and cryptographic operations in Azure Key Vault. This skill provides TypeScript SDK patterns that Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can adapt for secure Azure workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install @azure/keyvault-keys and @azure/identity for Node.js TypeScript projects.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential setup and KeyClient construction from a vault URL.
Provides examples for creating, listing, deleting, rotating, backing up, and restoring keys.
Covers CryptographyClient encryption, decryption, signing, verification, wrapping, and unwrapping examples.
Includes key rotation policy examples with expiration and lifetime action settings.
Includes related Azure Key Vault secret examples, including set, get, list, delete, backup, and restore.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not provide a tested application or reusable library.
Some examples cover Azure Key Vault secrets, although the skill name focuses on keys.
The examples assume Node.js because the Azure SDK packages shown are not browser supported.
Users must supply Azure identity, vault permissions, and production safety controls.

use_cases.title: Add Key Vault Keys To A Service
use_cases.description: Implement key creation, retrieval, and crypto client setup in a TypeScript backend service.

use_cases.title: Plan Key Rotation Workflows
use_cases.description: Draft rotation policy code and operational steps for Azure-managed cryptographic keys.

use_cases.title: Review Azure SDK Usage
use_cases.description: Compare existing Key Vault code against common SDK patterns for authentication, algorithms, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Key Client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a TypeScript project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show how to configure DefaultAzureCredential and create an Azure Key Vault KeyClient in TypeScript.

prompt_templates.title: Add Encryption And Decryption
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft TypeScript code that encrypts and decrypts data with CryptographyClient and RSA-OAEP.

prompt_templates.title: Design Key Rotation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a TypeScript key rotation workflow with expiration dates and an Azure Key Vault rotation policy.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Key Vault Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Azure Key Vault TypeScript code for unsafe logging, destructive operations, missing error handling, and excessive permissions.

output_examples.input: Create a TypeScript helper for Azure Key Vault key creation.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with required packages, credential setup, key client initialization, key creation options, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Review this Key Vault rotation approach for production readiness.
output: Flags purge operations for separate approval.
Suggests tests for rotation and recovery paths.

output_examples.input: Explain how to sign and verify messages with Key Vault.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step explanation covering hash creation, signing with a supported algorithm, verification, and safe handling of results.

best_practices: Use managed identities or DefaultAzureCredential with least-privilege Key Vault roles.
Avoid logging secret values, plaintext, ciphertext keys, backup blobs, or decrypted results.
Place purge, restore, and rotation operations behind explicit approval and audit controls.
anti_patterns: Do not copy examples that print secret.value into production logs or CI output.
Do not grant broad key operations when an application only needs encrypt, decrypt, sign, or verify.
Do not run purge examples against production vaults without a tested recovery and approval process.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown skill with TypeScript examples that users may adapt and run in their own projects.

faq.question: Which Azure package is the main focus?
faq.answer: The main package is @azure/keyvault-keys, with @azure/identity for authentication.

faq.question: Why does the audit mark the skill as medium risk?
faq.answer: The examples include sensitive cloud operations, such as logging a secret value and permanently purging deleted keys or secrets.

faq.question: Can this skill be used in browser applications?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that these Azure SDK examples are for Node.js environments.

faq.question: Does the skill include secret operations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes several Azure Key Vault secret examples even though the skill name focuses on keys.

faq.question: What should users check before running copied examples?
faq.answer: Users should verify vault permissions, remove sensitive logging, confirm the target vault, and protect destructive operations.
